one_line_takeaway: A SoK that re-frames LLM unlearning by the intention behind each method (true removal vs. behavioral suppression) and argues, via synthesized adversarial and relearning evidence, that popular GA-based 'removal' methods only suppress knowledge rather than erase it.
key_finding: The paper's headline claim is that removal-intended methods, above all Gradient Ascent (GA) and its variants, do NOT truly erase knowledge; they behave like suppression. Evidence synthesized from cited studies: (a) adversarial prompting recovers forgotten content (a GCG attack recovers 54% of forgetting knowledge; subsequence prompts still elicit correct answers; larger models are more vulnerable); (b) benign small-scale fine-tuning re-surfaces 'unlearned' knowledge, showing traces persist in weights; (c) mechanistically, GA loss simply increases output error whenever the forget data appears, so the model learns to treat forget data as an 'unlearning signal' that triggers feigned ignorance rather than removing the knowledge, which also explains over-generalization and the syntactic-similarity effect. The paper concludes no theoretical framework or reliable empirical test currently guarantees or verifies true removal, and argues behavioral suppression may be acceptable in practice while true removal should remain a research goal.
core_contribution: A Systematization-of-Knowledge (SoK) survey of machine unlearning for LLMs whose central original contribution is a new INTENTION-ORIENTED taxonomy. Instead of organizing methods by technical mechanism (as prior surveys do), the authors classify them by their 'second-level intention': (1) removal-intended unlearning, which aims to genuinely erase internal knowledge/training traces of the forget set (GA-based methods and model editing via task arithmetic), and (2) suppression-intended unlearning, which accepts the data may still be encoded but restricts outputs to behave as if forgotten (organized by the LLM component targeted: full-parameter fine-tuning, input space, hidden-representation space, output space). Table 1 lays out this two-level taxonomy. Building on it, the paper makes three further contributions: (a) a critical argument that GA-based 'removal' methods in practice only perform suppression; (b) a survey of evaluation metrics and benchmarks with their limitations; (c) a discussion of practical deployment gaps (utility preservation, sequential unlearning, scalability, poisoning, stage interference).
threat_model: The paper is motivated by privacy, copyright, and safety compliance rather than a single attacker. Data owners invoke the GDPR 'right to be forgotten' (Article 17), the EU AI Act (Article 53), and US Executive Order 14110 to demand removal of copyrighted/private/sensitive training data (e.g., the NYT v. OpenAI/Microsoft suit is cited); WMDP-style harmful bio/chemical knowledge must be filtered. Adversaries in the paper's own Section 7 try to recover supposedly-unlearned knowledge: adversarial-prompt / GCG suffix attacks, subsequence prompting, soft-token (embedding-space) attacks, and benign relearning/fine-tuning that reactivates hidden knowledge. Section 9 also raises a poisoning threat: a malicious user submitting a poisoned unlearning request to deliberately degrade model utility. The paper synthesizes these threats from cited work rather than mounting attacks itself.
limitations: Being a SoK, the paper contributes no new experiments or benchmark of its own; its central 'removal-is-actually-suppression' thesis rests entirely on synthesizing others' findings rather than on independent validation. The intention-based taxonomy is conceptually useful but hard to operationalize, since a method's 'intention' is inferred and current benchmarks cannot cleanly distinguish true removal from suppression. The authors themselves flag that: no theoretical tool traces/inverts the parameter changes induced by Df; there is no commercial unlearning product to ground real-world 'what should the user submit?' questions; benchmarks that fine-tune synthetic knowledge (TOFU, MUSE) conflate unlearning with catastrophic forgetting/overfitting; greedy-decoding evaluation underestimates retained knowledge; and even soft-token attacks are unreliable auditors (they can elicit content the model never saw, causing false positives). Scalability beyond 7B/8B models and sequential-request utility decay remain open.

datasets_used: TOFU benchmark (synthetic fictitious authors/books QA; requires fine-tuning the model on the synthetic corpus first; utility measured on retain set plus real-authors and world-knowledge QA sets) - reviewed, not run by the authors
MUSE benchmark (non-synthetic corpus outside the tested LLM's training data; emphasizes scalability and sequential unlearning) - reviewed
WMDP benchmark (harmful bio/chemical knowledge, multiple-choice format; utility via MMLU/LLMU and MT-Bench) - reviewed
RWKU benchmark (200 real-world famous entities; fill-in-the-blank, QA, and nine adversarial probe types; also tests neighboring-knowledge side effects) - reviewed
Derivative benchmarks mentioned: PerMU (robustness), R-TOFU (reasoning), LUME
quantitative_results: GCG adversarial-suffix attack (Yuan et al.) recovers 54% of forgetting knowledge with no access to model parameters
Sequential unlearning illustrative estimate: ~3% utility drop per step compounds to up to ~45% utility loss after 20 iterations (and per-step loss is often much larger than 3%)
Coreset effect (Pal et al.): a randomly selected 5% subset of the forget set yields unlearning performance comparable to the full set
UPCORE (Patil et al.): pruning 10%-30% of forget-set outliers reduces utility loss
Most surveyed experiments run on relatively small 7B/8B-parameter models, which the paper flags as a scalability gap
These numbers are all cited from surveyed works; the SoK reports no experimental results of its own
baselines_compared: N/A - survey/SoK with no experiments of its own; below are the representative method families it systematizes rather than empirical baselines

Removal-intended: Gradient Ascent (GA), Gradient Difference / retaining-set, Negative Preference Optimization (NPO, provably exponentially slower divergence than GA), gradient-conflict methods, second-order/SOUL methods, selective forgetting, Task Arithmetic model editing
Suppression-intended full-parameter: Inverted Hinge Loss (IHL), Obliviate KL-based loss, rejection fine-tuning ('I don't know'), incorrect-label fine-tuning (Who's Harry Potter)
Suppression-intended input space: In-Context Unlearning (ICUL), RAG-based unlearning, ALU agentic pipeline, embedding-corrupted prompts
Suppression-intended representation space: RMU (Representation Misdirection), SAE feature clamping, GRUN gated ReFT / LoRA modules, selective pruning/localization
Suppression-intended output space: delta-unlearning logit offset, logit-difference assistant-model subtraction, GUARD retrieval-based token blocking
